Query: What systematic reviews compare physiotherapy vs surgery for rotator cuff tears?
Response A:
## Evidence Summary
The retrieved documents collectively address the use of ultrasound-guided nerve blocks for postoperative pain management in total knee arthroplasty (TKA). One systematic review and meta-analysis of 30 RCTs found that adductor canal block (ACB) combined with local infiltration analgesia (LIA) provided superior pain relief and functional recovery compared to ACB alone [Doc 1]. A randomized controlled trial (RCT) demonstrated that adding a single-shot sciatic nerve block to ACB improved pain scores and reduced opioid consumption in the first 24 hours post-TKA [Doc 2]. Another RCT reported that a continuous femoral nerve block (FNB) provided better analgesia than a single-shot FNB, but with increased quadriceps weakness [Doc 3]. A prospective cohort study found that an ultrasound-guided iPACK block combined with ACB reduced posterior knee pain and opioid use compared to ACB alone [Doc 4]. One document is a narrative review that broadly discusses regional anesthesia techniques for TKA but does not present original data [Doc 5].

## Sources Used
• [Doc 1] Journal of Arthroplasty | EL 1 | Tier 1 | 2022 — Systematic review and meta-analysis of 30 RCTs comparing ACB+LIA vs ACB alone for TKA.
• [Doc 2] Regional Anesthesia & Pain Medicine | EL 1 | Tier 1 | 2021 — RCT evaluating addition of sciatic nerve block to ACB for TKA.
• [Doc 3] Anesthesia & Analgesia | EL 1 | Tier 1 | 2020 — RCT comparing continuous vs single-shot FNB for TKA.
• [Doc 4] Journal of Clinical Anesthesia | EL 3 | Tier 2 | 2023 — Prospective cohort study on iPACK block combined with ACB for TKA.
• [Doc 5] Current Opinion in Anaesthesiology | EL 5 | Tier 2 | 2022 — Narrative review of regional anesthesia techniques for TKA; not directly relevant to the query as it provides no new evidence.

## Evidence Quality Assessment
MODERATE — Two EL-1 documents (RCTs) and one EL-1 systematic review directly address the query, but the evidence is limited to specific nerve block combinations and does not cover all commonly used ultrasound-guided blocks for TKA.

## Gap Statement
The retrieved evidence does not directly compare all major ultrasound-guided nerve block techniques (e.g., ACB vs FNB vs sciatic nerve block) head-to-head in a single study, nor does it provide long-term functional outcomes or complication rates beyond the immediate postoperative period.
